[Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") (Additional languages for language programmes)

(abbreviated as **LEI**;[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Universiteit Leiden*) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Leiden](/wiki/Leiden "Leiden"), Netherlands. Established in 1575 by [William, Prince of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") as a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") institution,[[12]](#cite_note-12) it holds the distinction of being the oldest surviving university in the Netherlands.[[a]](#cite_note-13)

**Leiden University** (abbreviated as **LEI**;[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Universiteit Leiden*) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Leiden](/wiki/Leiden "Leiden"), Netherlands. Established in 1575 by [William, Prince of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") as a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") institution,[[12]](#cite_note-12) it holds the distinction of being the oldest surviving university in the Netherlands.[[a]](#cite_note-13)

During the [Dutch Golden Age](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age "Dutch Golden Age") scholars from around Europe were attracted to the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") for its climate of intellectual tolerance. Individuals such as [René Descartes](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Descartes "René Descartes"), [Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt"), [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens"), [Hugo Grotius](/wiki/Hugo_Grotius "Hugo Grotius"), [Benedictus Spinoza](/wiki/Benedictus_Spinoza "Benedictus Spinoza"), and later [Baron d'Holbach](/wiki/Baron_d%27Holbach "Baron d'Holbach") were active in Leiden and environs.

The university has seven academic faculties and over fifty subject departments, housing more than forty national and international research institutes. Its historical primary campus consists of several buildings spread over Leiden, while a second campus located in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") houses a liberal arts college ([Leiden University College The Hague](/wiki/Leiden_University_College_The_Hague "Leiden University College The Hague")) and several of its faculties. It is a member of the [Coimbra Group](/wiki/Coimbra_Group "Coimbra Group"), the [Europaeum](/wiki/Europaeum "Europaeum"), and a founding member of the [League of European Research Universities](/wiki/League_of_European_Research_Universities "League of European Research Universities").

The university has produced twenty-six [Spinoza Prize Laureates](/wiki/Spinoza_Prize "Spinoza Prize") and sixteen [Nobel Laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates "List of Nobel laureates"). Members of the [Dutch royal family](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau") such as Queen [Juliana](/wiki/Juliana_of_the_Netherlands "Juliana of the Netherlands"), Queen [Beatrix](/wiki/Beatrix_of_the_Netherlands "Beatrix of the Netherlands"), and King [Willem-Alexander](/wiki/Willem-Alexander_of_the_Netherlands "Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands") are alumni, and ten [prime ministers](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") of the Netherlands including [Mark Rutte](/wiki/Mark_Rutte "Mark Rutte"). US president [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams") also studied at the university.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Foundation and early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation and early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/William_the_Silent_16th_century.jpg/250px-William_the_Silent_16th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_the_Silent_16th_century.jpg)

[William the Silent](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent"), founder of the university, in the 16th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Academia_Leidensis_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Academia_Leidensis_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Academia_Leidensis_(cropped).jpg)

The academy building of Leiden University in 1614.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Anatomical_theatre_Leiden.jpg/250px-Anatomical_theatre_Leiden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anatomical_theatre_Leiden.jpg)

[Leiden anatomical theatre](/wiki/Leiden_anatomical_theatre "Leiden anatomical theatre")

In 1575, the emerging [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") did not have universities in its northern heartland. The other universities in the [Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands") were the [University of Leuven](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven") and the [University of Douai](/wiki/University_of_Douai "University of Douai"), located in an area under firm Spanish control. [Prince William](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") founded Leiden University to give the Northern Netherlands an institution that could educate its citizens in religion and provide the government with educated men in all fields.[[14]](#cite_note-WOI-15)[[15]](#cite_note-:0-16) It is said the choice fell on Leiden as a reward for the heroic [defence of Leiden](/wiki/Siege_of_Leiden "Siege of Leiden") against Spanish attacks in 1574. The name of [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), William's adversary, appears on the official foundation certificate as he was still the *de jure* [count of Holland](/wiki/Count_of_Holland "Count of Holland").[[16]](#cite_note-17) Philip II forbade all his subjects to study in Leiden.

The new institution was initially located in the Convent of Saint Barbara, then moved to the Faliede Bagijn Church in 1577 (now the location of the university museum) and in 1581 to a former convent of [Cistercian nuns](/wiki/Cistercian_nuns "Cistercian nuns"), a site which it still occupies, though the original building was destroyed by a fire in 1616.[[14]](#cite_note-WOI-15)

Leiden University's reputation was created in part by the presence of scholars such as [Justus Lipsius](/wiki/Justus_Lipsius "Justus Lipsius"), [Joseph Scaliger](/wiki/Joseph_Justus_Scaliger "Joseph Justus Scaliger"), [Franciscus Gomarus](/wiki/Franciscus_Gomarus "Franciscus Gomarus"), [Hugo Grotius](/wiki/Grotius "Grotius"), [Jacobus Arminius](/wiki/Jacobus_Arminius "Jacobus Arminius"), [Daniel Heinsius](/wiki/Daniel_Heinsius "Daniel Heinsius"), and [Gerhard Johann Vossius](/wiki/Gerhard_Johann_Vossius "Gerhard Johann Vossius") within fifty years of its founding. By the 1640s, over five hundred students were enrolled from all across Europe, making it the largest Protestant university.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[15]](#cite_note-:0-16) [Baruch Spinoza](/wiki/Baruch_Spinoza "Baruch Spinoza") discovered Descartes's work partly at Leiden University,[[18]](#cite_note-19) which he visited for periods of study multiple times.[[19]](#cite_note-20) In the 18th century, [Jacobus Gronovius](/wiki/Jakob_Gronovius "Jakob Gronovius"), [Herman Boerhaave](/wiki/Herman_Boerhaave "Herman Boerhaave"), [Tiberius Hemsterhuis](/wiki/Tiberius_Hemsterhuis "Tiberius Hemsterhuis"), and [David Ruhnken](/wiki/David_Ruhnken "David Ruhnken") were among the renowned academics of the university.

In 1896, the [Zeeman effect](/wiki/Zeeman_effect "Zeeman effect") was discovered at the institution by [Pieter Zeeman](/wiki/Pieter_Zeeman "Pieter Zeeman") and shortly afterward explained by [Hendrik Antoon Lorentz](/wiki/Hendrik_Antoon_Lorentz "Hendrik Antoon Lorentz").[[20]](#cite_note-21) In the world's first university low-temperature laboratory, Professor [Heike Kamerlingh Onnes](/wiki/Heike_Kamerlingh_Onnes "Heike Kamerlingh Onnes") achieved a temperature only one degree above [absolute zero](/wiki/Absolute_zero "Absolute zero"). In 1908, he was also the first to succeed in [liquifying helium](/wiki/Liquid_helium "Liquid helium") and has played a role in the discovery of [superconductivity](/wiki/Superconductivity "Superconductivity") in metals.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

### Modern day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern day")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Leiden_1610.jpg/250px-Leiden_1610.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leiden_1610.jpg)

Leiden University Library in 1610

The [University Library](/wiki/Leiden_University_Library "Leiden University Library") has more than 5.2 million books and fifty thousand journals. It also has collections of Western and Oriental [manuscripts](/wiki/Manuscript "Manuscript"), printed books, archives, prints, drawings, photographs, maps, and [atlases](/wiki/Atlas "Atlas"). It houses the world's largest collections on [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [the Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), collected by the [Scaliger Institute](/wiki/Leiden_University_Library#Scaliger_Institute "Leiden University Library") which studies various aspects of knowledge transmissions and ideas through texts and images from antiquity to the present day. In 2005, the manuscript of [Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") on the quantum theory of the monatomic ideal gas (the [Einstein-Bose condensation](/wiki/Einstein-Bose_condensation "Einstein-Bose condensation")) was discovered in one of Leiden's libraries.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

#### Partnerships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Partnerships")]

In 2012 Leiden entered into a strategic alliance with [Delft University of Technology](/wiki/Delft_University_of_Technology "Delft University of Technology") and [Erasmus University Rotterdam](/wiki/Erasmus_University_Rotterdam "Erasmus University Rotterdam") for the universities to increase the quality of their research and teaching. The university is also the unofficial home of the [Bilderberg Group](/wiki/Bilderberg_Group "Bilderberg Group"), a meeting of high-level political and economic figures from North America and Europe. Leiden University partnered with [Duke University School of Law](/wiki/Duke_University_School_of_Law "Duke University School of Law") starting in 2017 to run a joint summer program on global and transnational law from its Hague campus.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Location and buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Location and buildings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Leiden_-_Rapenburg_-_universiteit.JPG/250px-Leiden_-_Rapenburg_-_universiteit.JPG)](/wiki/File:Leiden_-_Rapenburg_-_universiteit.JPG)

The academy building of Leiden University in modern days

The university has no central campus; its buildings are spread over the city. Some buildings, like the Gravensteen, are very old, while Van Steenis, Lipsius and Gorlaeus are much more modern.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Among the institutions affiliated with the university are The [KITLV](/wiki/KITLV "KITLV") or Royal Netherlands Institute of Southeast Asian and Caribbean Studies (founded in 1851), the Leiden Observatory 1633; the Natural History Museum, with a very complete anatomical cabinet; the *[Rijksmuseum van Oudheden](/wiki/Rijksmuseum_van_Oudheden "Rijksmuseum van Oudheden")* (National Museum of Antiquities), with especially valuable [Egyptian](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") and Indian departments; a museum of Dutch antiquities from the earliest times; and three ethnographical museums, of which the nucleus was [Philipp Franz von Siebold](/wiki/Philipp_Franz_von_Siebold "Philipp Franz von Siebold")'s Japanese collections. The [anatomical](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") and [pathological](/wiki/Pathology "Pathology") laboratories of the university are modern, and the museums of geology and [mineralogy](/wiki/Mineralogy "Mineralogy") have been restored.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Hortus Botanicus](/wiki/Hortus_Botanicus_Leiden "Hortus Botanicus Leiden") (botanical garden) is the oldest [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden") in the Netherlands and one of the oldest in the world. Plants from all over the world have been carefully cultivated here by experts for more than four centuries. The Clusius garden (a reconstruction), the 18th-century Orangery with its monumental tub plants, the rare collection of historical trees hundreds of years old, the Japanese [Siebold Memorial Museum](/wiki/Siebold_Memorial_Museum "Siebold Memorial Museum") symbolising the historical link between East and West, the tropical greenhouses with their world-class plant collections, and the central square and Conservatory exhibiting exotic plants from South Africa and southern Europe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### *Zweetkamertje*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Zweetkamertje")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Zweetkamertje4.JPG/250px-Zweetkamertje4.JPG)](/wiki/File:Zweetkamertje4.JPG)

The "Sweat Room" ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Zweetkamertje*) is a small chamber in the university's Academy Building, traditionally used by doctoral candidates awaiting the results of their PhD defenses. The room is renowned for its walls covered with the signatures of graduates, including notable figures such as [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela"), and members of the Dutch royal family.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Originally serving as a meeting room for university curators, the Zweetkamertje became associated with doctoral examinations in the 18th century. Candidates would wait in this room before defending their dissertations, often experiencing considerable anxiety, hence the name. Over time, it became customary for successful candidates to inscribe their names on the walls as a rite of passage.[[25]](#cite_note-26) The tradition of signing the Zweetkamertje walls is a cherished aspect of Leiden University's heritage. The room features thousands of signatures, including those of honorary doctorate recipients. In 2014, a crowdfunding campaign successfully raised funds to restore the room's deteriorating plaster, ensuring the preservation of this unique historical record.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### Campus The Hague

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Campus The Hague")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Wijnhaven_Lecture_Hall.jpg/250px-Wijnhaven_Lecture_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wijnhaven_Lecture_Hall.jpg)

A lecture hall in the Leiden University campus in the Hague.

In 1998, the university has expanded to The Hague which has become home to [Campus The Hague](/wiki/Campus_The_Hague "Campus The Hague"), with six of the seven faculties represented and exclusive home to the Faculty of Governance and Global Affairs, International Studies and [Leiden University College The Hague](/wiki/Leiden_University_College_The_Hague "Leiden University College The Hague"), a liberal arts and sciences college. Here, the university offers academic courses in the fields of law, political science, public administration and medicine. It occupied a number of buildings in the centre of the city, including a college building at [Lange Voorhout](/wiki/Lange_Voorhout "Lange Voorhout"), before moving into the new 'Wijnhaven' building on Turfmarkt in 2016.

The Faculty of Governance and Global Affairs was established in 2011, together with the University College, and one of the largest programmes of the Faculty of Humanities, International Studies.

Since 2017 [Leiden University Medical Center](/wiki/Leiden_University_Medical_Center "Leiden University Medical Center") also has a branch at Campus The Hague.

## Academic profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Academic profile")]

### Undergraduate studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Undergraduate studies")]

Most of the university's departments offer their degree programme(s). Undergraduate programmes lead to either a B.A., B.Sc., or [LL.B.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Laws "Bachelor of Laws") degree. Other degrees, such as the [B.Eng.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Engineering "Bachelor of Engineering") or [B.F.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Fine_Arts "Bachelor of Fine Arts"), are not awarded at Leiden University.

### Graduate studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Graduate studies")]

Students can choose from a range of graduate programmes. Most of the above-mentioned undergraduate programmes can be continued with either a general or a specialised graduate program. Leiden University offers more than 100 graduate programs leading to either [MA](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts"), [MSc](/wiki/Master_of_Science "Master of Science"), [MPhil](/wiki/Master_of_Philosophy "Master of Philosophy"), or [LLM degrees](/wiki/Master_of_Laws "Master of Laws"). The MPhil is the most advanced graduate degree and is awarded by select university departments (mostly in the fields of Arts, Social Sciences, Archeology, Philosophy, and Theology). Admission to these programmes is highly selective and primarily aimed at those students opting for an academic career or before going into law or medicine. Traditionally, the MPhil degree enabled its holder to teach at the university levels as an associate professor.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Faculteit_der_Sociale_Wetenschappen_Leiden.jpg/250px-Faculteit_der_Sociale_Wetenschappen_Leiden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Faculteit_der_Sociale_Wetenschappen_Leiden.jpg)

The [Pieter de la Court](/wiki/Pieter_de_la_Court "Pieter de la Court")-building, the main building of the Faculty of Social and Behavioural Sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Doctorate programmers")]

In addition, most departments, affiliated (research) institutes, or faculties offer doctorate programmes or positions, leading to the Ph.D. degree. Most of the Ph.D. programmes offered by the university are concentrated in several research schools or institutes.

### Notable alumni and professors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notable alumni and professors")]

Main article: [List of Leiden University people](/wiki/List_of_Leiden_University_people "List of Leiden University people")

Of the 110 [Spinoza Prize](/wiki/Spinoza_Prize "Spinoza Prize") laureates (the highest scientific award of The Netherlands), twenty-eight were granted to professors of Leiden University. Literary historian [Frits van Oostrom](/wiki/Frits_van_Oostrom "Frits van Oostrom") was the first professor of Leiden to be granted the Spinoza award for his work on developing the NLCM centre (Dutch literature and culture in the Middle Ages) into a top research centre. Other Spinoza Prize winners are:

* [Frederik Kortlandt](/wiki/Frederik_Kortlandt "Frederik Kortlandt"), linguist
* [Hendrik Lenstra](/wiki/Hendrik_Lenstra "Hendrik Lenstra"), mathematician
* [Carlo Beenakker](/wiki/Carlo_Beenakker "Carlo Beenakker"), physicist
* [Jan Zaanen](/wiki/Jan_Zaanen "Jan Zaanen"), physicist
* [Dirk Bouwmeester](/wiki/Dirk_Bouwmeester "Dirk Bouwmeester"), physicist
* [Michel Orrit](/wiki/Michel_Orrit "Michel Orrit"), physicist
* [Ewine van Dishoeck](/wiki/Ewine_van_Dishoeck "Ewine van Dishoeck"), astronomer
* [Marijn Franx](/wiki/Marijn_Franx "Marijn Franx"), astronomer
* [Alexander Tielens](/wiki/Alexander_Tielens "Alexander Tielens"), astronomer
* [Els Goulmy](/wiki/Els_Goulmy "Els Goulmy"), transplantation biologist
* [Marinus van IJzendoorn](/wiki/Marinus_van_IJzendoorn "Marinus van IJzendoorn"), pedagogue
* [Wil Roebroeks](/wiki/Wil_Roebroeks "Wil Roebroeks"), archeologist
* [Corinne Hofman](/wiki/Corinne_Hofman "Corinne Hofman"), archeologist
* [Michel Ferrari](/wiki/Michel_Ferrari "Michel Ferrari"), neurologist
* [Ineke Sluiter](/wiki/Ineke_Sluiter "Ineke Sluiter"), classicist
* [Naomi Ellemers](/wiki/Naomi_Ellemers "Naomi Ellemers"), social psychologist
* [Aad van der Vaart](/wiki/Aad_van_der_Vaart "Aad van der Vaart"), statistician
* [Eveline Crone](/wiki/Eveline_Crone "Eveline Crone"), cognitive psychologist
* [Carsten de Dreu](/wiki/Carsten_de_Dreu "Carsten de Dreu"), organisation psychologist
* [Sjaak Neefjes](/wiki/Jacques_Neefjes "Jacques Neefjes"), chemical immunologist
* [Maria Yazdanbakhsh](/wiki/Maria_Yazdanbakhsh "Maria Yazdanbakhsh"), parasitologist

The [Stevin Prize](/wiki/Stevin_Prize "Stevin Prize") laureates who have achieved exceptional success in knowledge exchange and impact for society include the following Leiden professors: health psychologist [Andrea Evers](/w/index.php?title=Andrea_Evers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andrea Evers (page does not exist)"), immunology technologist [Ton Schumacher](/w/index.php?title=Ton_Schumacher&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ton Schumacher (page does not exist)") and psychologist [Judi Mesman](/w/index.php?title=Judi_Mesman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Judi Mesman (page does not exist)").[[48]](#cite_note-49)

Other notable Leiden researchers were the Arabist and Islam expert [Christiaan Snouck Hurgronje](/wiki/Christiaan_Snouck_Hurgronje "Christiaan Snouck Hurgronje"), the law expert [Cornelis van Vollenhoven](/wiki/Cornelis_van_Vollenhoven "Cornelis van Vollenhoven") and historian [Johan Huizinga](/wiki/Johan_Huizinga "Johan Huizinga"), all during the 1920s and 1930s. [Martinus Beijerinck](/wiki/Martinus_Beijerinck "Martinus Beijerinck"), one of the founders of virology, finished his Ph.D. at Leiden in 1877.

### Nobel laureates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Nobel laureates")]

[Kamerlingh Onnes](/wiki/Heike_Kamerlingh_Onnes "Heike Kamerlingh Onnes") was awarded the Nobel Prize for Physics in 1913. Three other professors received the Nobel Prize for their research performed at Universiteit Leiden: [Hendrik Antoon Lorentz](/wiki/Hendrik_Antoon_Lorentz "Hendrik Antoon Lorentz") and [Pieter Zeeman](/wiki/Pieter_Zeeman "Pieter Zeeman") received the Nobel Prize for their pioneering work in the field of optical and electronic phenomena, and the physiologist [Willem Einthoven](/wiki/Willem_Einthoven "Willem Einthoven") for his invention of the string galvanometer, which among other things, enabled the development of electrocardiography.

Nobel laureates associated with Leiden include the physicists [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein"), [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi"), and [Paul Ehrenfest](/wiki/Paul_Ehrenfest "Paul Ehrenfest"). Other Leiden-affiliated Nobel laureates include [Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff](/wiki/Jacobus_Henricus_van_%27t_Hoff "Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff"), [Johannes Diderik van der Waals](/wiki/Johannes_Diderik_van_der_Waals "Johannes Diderik van der Waals"), [Tobias Asser](/wiki/Tobias_Asser "Tobias Asser"), [Albert Szent-Györgyi](/wiki/Albert_Szent-Gy%C3%B6rgyi "Albert Szent-Györgyi"), [Igor Tamm](/wiki/Igor_Tamm "Igor Tamm"), [Jan Tinbergen](/wiki/Jan_Tinbergen "Jan Tinbergen"), [Nikolaas Tinbergen](/wiki/Nikolaas_Tinbergen "Nikolaas Tinbergen"), [Tjalling Koopmans](/wiki/Tjalling_Koopmans "Tjalling Koopmans"), [Nicolaas Bloembergen](/wiki/Nicolaas_Bloembergen "Nicolaas Bloembergen"), and [Niels Jerne](/wiki/Niels_Jerne "Niels Jerne").[[49]](#cite_note-50)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leiden_University&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** During the period of the [Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), which included the present-day Netherlands, the [University of Leuven](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven") (1425–1797) was the oldest university. After the separation of the northern Netherlands, the University of Leiden (founded in 1575) became the first and oldest university in the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") and later in its successor, the [Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "Kingdom of the Netherlands").
